what_it_does: Proposes a staged multi-part response system in which one brain model emits a single user-facing reply as sequential parts: an instant first part with no dependencies, and later parts enriched by concurrently resolving auxiliary-role outputs, using declarative thought-shape templates and mode-driven selection.
observations: Establishes the vocabulary for the concurrent-cognition series, noting no prior 01–03 existed.
Frames a staged reply as one continuous stream to the operator, with parts as a production detail rather than a presentation one.
Reuses the existing reactive scheduler primitive—a part fires when its input addresses resolve—applied inside a single turn.
Introduces a THOUGHT_SHAPES registry of declarative, mode-selected answer shapes (e.g., acknowledge→retrieve→synthesise→offer, and direct).
Adds a brevity-judge role to bypass staging for short or trivial messages.
Proposes a per-mode SLOT_BUDGET as an attention cap on parts, concurrent roles, and total tokens.
Mirrors the existing _voice_stream NDJSON transport for the staged-response endpoint.
Insists that verb dispatch reuse the existing whitelist and governance (E6) so staging cannot become a side-door.
Explicitly separates backend function from frontend form as two mandatory lanes.
standouts: The core relationship 'a part is a node, a role-output is an address' reuses the canvas scheduler intra-turn.
Mode is the single dial that decides staging, shape, roles, judge bias, and budget.
Real parallelism is conditioned on enrichers binding a distinct model/endpoint, not falling back to the brain.
Governance reuse is treated as non-negotiable: staged parts must dispatch through the same whitelist.
conventions: Follows registry-is-truth with single-source dicts (THOUGHT_SHAPES, ROLE_REGISTRY, MODE_BUDGETS).
Follows declarative-template style similar to panels/.
Follows fail-loud design for role errors, drift checks, and config validation.
Follows additive, git-reversible extension rather than replacing chat().
Breaks from today's single-shot, non-streaming chat() by introducing concurrent model calls and an intra-turn address space.
concerns: Many implementation choices are explicitly left as open questions (scheduler reuse, persistence, on-fail policy, judge cost).
The 'instant Part 1' claim still requires a model call and is only truly parallel if distinct models are bound.
The brevity judge adds a hot-path model call whose benefit is flagged as unmeasured.
Verb dispatch across multiple parts could complicate confirmation folding and action/proposal return shape.
Shape authoring by the brain is mentioned as future work without concrete governance details.
notes: A design proposal for making one AI reply feel like a continuous human utterance by staging instant and enriched parts, all gated by presence mode and built on existing scheduler/governance primitives.
questions: Will the in-turn scheduler be a lightweight runner or reuse the runtime graph scheduler verbatim?
How are role outputs formatted and injected into each part's prompt?
What is the exact config slot for overriding the per-mode shape binding?
How is max_tokens_total enforced across multiple part calls?
Will intra-turn role/part addresses persist to the store or remain ephemeral?
How does the FE render a proposal card inside a single unbroken bubble?
How will the voice circuit consume staged parts instead of sentence-chunking a full chat() reply?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose matches content: it is a proposal establishing vocabulary and design for staged multi-part responses.
apparent_themes: Reactive scheduling scaled down to a single turn.
intention_signals: To make the assistant's replies feel like one natural, continuous utterance—an immediate acknowledgement followed by substance gathered in the background—while reusing the repo's existing scheduler, role, mode, and governance primitives rather than inventing parallel machinery.
novelty: Applying the canvas node/address scheduler relationship inside one chat turn; a declarative thought-shape registry; a per-mode slot budget as an attention cap; and a brevity-judge role as a staging gate.
contribution: A design vocabulary and registry pattern for mode-gated, multi-part replies that reuses existing scheduler and governance primitives and explicitly separates backend generation from frontend rendering.
summary_for_embedding: A design proposal for a staged multi-part chat response that emits an instant first part and enriches later parts via concurrently resolving auxiliary roles, using declarative thought-shape templates, mode-driven selection, and a per-mode slot budget.
intention_for_embedding: To establish the conceptual substrate for the concurrent-cognition series by mapping the existing reactive scheduler and role registry onto intra-turn reply staging.
